questions.scenario: The Orders API runs on Cloud Run and needs to call Secret Manager. What is the safer starting point?

options.text: Attach a runtime service account to Cloud Run and grant it the needed IAM role.
Bake a service account key into the container image.
Paste a human refresh token into an environment variable.
Use the billing account as a password.

questions.explanation: GCP runtimes can use attached service accounts without storing a long-lived key in the app.

questions.scenario: A GitHub Actions workflow needs to deploy to GCP. The team wants to avoid storing a service account key in GitHub secrets. What should they evaluate?

options.text: Workload Identity Federation with a deploy service account and narrow IAM.
A permanent Owner key copied into every repository.
A database password reused as a GCP credential.
Removing IAM policies from the project.

questions.explanation: Workload Identity Federation is designed to let external workloads authenticate without maintaining service account keys.

questions.scenario: Why are service account keys a risky fallback?

options.text: They are long-lived credentials if not controlled by policy.
They can be copied outside GCP.
They must be stored, rotated, and monitored carefully.
They automatically have no permissions, so leaks are harmless.

questions.explanation: A leaked key can authenticate as the service account and use its permissions. Prefer attached identities, impersonation, or federation when possible.
